1: 'A shoot will come up from the stump of Jesse, and a branch from his roots will bear fruit' — the perfect continuity with Isaiah 10:33-34: Assyria's great forest is felled, but from the stump of Jesse (David's father) a shoot (choter, a young twig/sapling) grows. Jesse rather than David as the root deliberately goes back before the Davidic monarchy — the new king comes not from the corrupt royal succession but from the original stock. The 'branch' (netser, shoot/sprout) has a possible connection to the town name Nazareth (natsrath, from netser): Matthew 2:23 cites 'He will be called a Nazarene' as fulfillment of prophecy, possibly alluding to this 'branch' text. 'Bear fruit' (yifre, will bear fruit) from roots that seemed dead — the stump theology of 6:13 made productive.
2: 'The Spirit of the LORD will rest on him — the Spirit of wisdom and understanding, the Spirit of counsel and strength, the Spirit of knowledge and the fear of the LORD' — the seven-fold Spirit: (1) the Spirit of YHWH (the comprehensive designation), then six in pairs: (2) wisdom (hochmah) + (3) understanding (binah), (4) counsel (etsah) + (5) strength (gevurah), (6) knowledge (da'at) + (7) fear of YHWH (yir'at Yhwh). The number seven (completeness) and the three pairs (wisdom-practical, counsel-executive, knowledge-devotional) describe the complete endowment of the ideal king. Revelation 1:4; Isaiah 3:1; Isaiah 4:5; 5:6 applies the seven-fold Spirit to the divine presence before God's throne. John 1:32-33 records the Spirit resting (meno, abiding) on Jesus at his baptism — the 'rest' (nuach) verb of Isaiah 11:2.
3: 'He will take delight in the fear of the LORD. He will not judge by what his eyes see or decide cases by what his ears hear' — the messianic king's epistemology: judgment by inner discernment (divine wisdom) rather than outward appearance (eyes and ears). The delight in the fear of YHWH (vaharicho beyir'at Yhwh, and his delight/breath will be in the fear of YHWH) is the character of the sage who operates from covenant wisdom rather than political calculation. The contrast with human judges who can be bribed (appearance-based judgment) or deceived (hearing false testimony) marks the Davidic ideal never achieved by any historical king.
4: 'But with righteousness he will judge the poor and with fairness he will decide for the humble of the earth; he will strike the earth with the rod of his mouth, and with the breath of his lips he will put the wicked to death' — the specific beneficiaries of messianic judgment: the poor (dallim) and humble (anavei erets). The instruments of his power: 'the rod of his mouth' (shevet pihu, the rod/staff of his mouth = his word as a weapon) and 'the breath of his lips' (ruach sefatav, the spirit/breath of his lips). Revelation 19:15 directly applies these images to the returning Christ: 'Out of his mouth comes a sharp sword with which to strike down the nations.' 2 Thessalonians 2:8 applies the 'breath of his mouth' to the destruction of the lawless one at Christ's coming.
5: 'Righteousness will be the belt around his waist, and faithfulness the girdle around his hips' — the royal investiture garments made moral: the belt (ezor, the sash worn around the waist for dignity and identification of status) is tzedaqah (righteousness) and the girdle around the loins is emunah (faithfulness/trustworthiness). Ephesians 6:14 applies this to the armor of God: 'Stand firm then, with the belt of truth buckled around your waist, with the breastplate of righteousness in place.' The contrast with the Zion-women's ornamental belt-and-sash (Isaiah 3:20-24, stripped as judgment) is striking: what the unfaithful women wore as adornment, the messianic king wears as his very character.
6: 'The wolf will live peacefully with the lamb, and the leopard will lie down with the young goat; the calf and the young lion and the fatling will be together, and a little child will lead them' — the peaceable kingdom: predator-prey reversal as the eschatological sign of new creation. Wolf (ze'ev) with lamb (keves), leopard (namer) with young goat (gedi), calf (egel) with young lion (kefir) — three natural predator-prey pairs now cohabiting. 'A little child will lead them' (yeled qatan noheg bam, a little child leads them) — the most vulnerable human being (a small child who cannot defend itself) safely directs the formerly dangerous animals. The child echoes the 'child born to us' of Isaiah 9:6, suggesting the messianic presence enables the peaceable kingdom.
7: 'The cow and the bear will graze together; their calves will lie down side by side; and the lion will eat straw like the ox' — the dietary transformation: the bear (dov) grazing with the cow (parah), their young ones together, and the lion eating straw (teven, the leftover dry stalks after threshing). The lion eating straw is the most astonishing reversal — the apex predator converted to a herbivore. Genesis 1:29-30 records the original diet as plants for all living things; only after the flood (Genesis 9:3) does YHWH permit meat-eating. The peaceable kingdom is thus a restoration of pre-fall Eden rather than a new invention — creation returned to its original design.
8: 'The nursing infant will play at the viper's burrow, and the toddler will reach his hand into the adder's den' — the snake imagery completes the Eden-reversal: the most primal human fear (the serpent of Gen 3) is defanged in the messianic kingdom. 'Nursing infant' (yoneq, the suckling) playing at the viper's (pethen) hole and the 'toddler' (gamul, the newly weaned child) reaching into the adder's (ef'eh) den. Both venomous snakes — the pethen is the cobra of Egypt, the ef'eh is the desert viper. The child who formerly could not survive snake contact now plays at their dens without harm. The curse of Genesis 3:15 ('it will strike your heel') is reversed.
9: 'Nothing will harm or destroy anywhere on my holy mountain, for the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the LORD as completely as the waters cover the sea' — the 'why' behind the peaceable kingdom: the universal knowledge of YHWH (da'at Yhwh) is the foundation. The same 'da'at' that was the cause of Israel's judgment ('destroyed from lack of knowledge,' Hosea 4:6) now fills the earth as completely as water covers the sea — an image of total, pervasive saturation. The comparison 'as the waters cover the sea bed' (kamayim layam mechasim) describes the impossibility of the land being uncovered: the sea bottom is never exposed. Habakkuk 2:14 uses the same phrase: 'the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of the LORD as the waters cover the sea.'
10: 'In that day the Root of Jesse will stand as a banner for the peoples; the nations will seek him, and his resting place will be filled with glory' — the second major designation: the 'Root of Jesse' (shoresh Yishai, the root/stock of Jesse) rather than the 'shoot from Jesse's stump' (v1). The Root is the source from which the stump grows — the king is not only the descendant of Jesse but his root, his origin. Paul cites this verse in Romans 15:12 as the scriptural promise of Gentile inclusion: 'the Root of Jesse will spring up, one who will arise to rule over the nations; in him the Gentiles will hope' (citing the LXX which has 'rule' and 'hope in him'). The banner/signal (nes, standard raised to summon troops or nations) standing for the peoples (le'ammim) draws them to YHWH's dwelling place.
11: 'In that day the Lord will extend his hand a second time to reclaim the remnant of his people who are left — from Assyria, from Egypt, from Pathros, from Cush, from Elam, from Shinar, from Hamath, and from the islands of the sea' — the second Exodus: YHWH extending his hand 'a second time' (shenit, a second time) to reclaim (liqnot, to acquire/redeem) the remnant (she'ar, the residue/remnant) of his people scattered across all the known world. Eight geographical regions are listed: east (Assyria, Elam, Shinar/Babylon), south (Egypt, Pathros/Upper Egypt, Cush/Ethiopia), north (Hamath), and west ('islands of the sea' = Mediterranean coast and islands). The comprehensive geographic list covers every direction of dispersion.
12: 'He will raise a banner for the nations and gather the exiled of Israel; he will assemble the scattered of Judah from the four corners of the earth' — the eschatological ingathering: the banner (nes) of v10 is raised for the nations to signal the gathering of Israel's exiles. Both the northern kingdom ('exiled of Israel,' nidchei Yisrael) and the southern ('scattered of Judah,' nefutsot Yehudah) are gathered. 'From the four corners of the earth' (mearba kanfot ha'arets, from the four wings/corners of the earth) — the comprehensive directional gathering, anticipating the ingathering of the Jewish diaspora from all directions. Ezekiel 37:21-22 (the valley of dry bones) and Zechariah 10:8-10 develop the same second-Exodus theology.
13: 'Ephraim's jealousy will come to an end, and those who antagonise Judah will be cut off; Ephraim will not envy Judah, and Judah will not vex Ephraim' — the healing of the north-south division: the rival jealousy (qin'at Efraim, the jealousy of Ephraim) and the antagonism of Judah (tsorerai Yehudah) — the north-south tension that erupted in the kingdom division of 931 BCE and persisted through the Syro-Ephraimite conflict (Isaiah 7:1-9; Isaiah 9:8-21) — will be resolved in the messianic age. The two halves of the covenant people unified. Ezekiel 37:15-23 (the two sticks becoming one in Ezekiel's hand) develops this same north-south reunification promise.
14: 'Together they will swoop down on the Philistines to the west and jointly plunder the peoples of the east; they will reach out against Edom and Moab, and the Ammonites will submit to them' — the reunited Israel's military power directed outward: the traditional enemies (Philistines to the west, Edom/Moab/Ammon to the east and southeast) now subdued. The reunion of north and south produces military strength that neither had separately. This is the fulfillment of the Davidic ideal of a united kingdom dominating the surrounding nations — a reality briefly achieved under David and Solomon but lost at the kingdom split.
15: 'The LORD will dry up the gulf of the Egyptian Sea and wave his hand over the Euphrates with his scorching wind, breaking it into seven streams so that men can cross it in sandals' — the second Exodus geography: YHWH drying up the 'tongue' of Egypt's sea (leshon yam-Mitsrayim, the gulf/tongue of the Egyptian Sea = the Gulf of Suez, the arm of the Red Sea) as he dried the Red Sea for Moses (Exod 14). Then he breaks the Euphrates (the barrier between Mesopotamia and the promised land) into seven wadis crossable on foot. The new exodus reverses both barriers: the sea that blocked escape from Egypt and the great river that blocked return from Babylon.
16: 'There will be a highway from Assyria for the remnant of his people who survive, just as there was for Israel when they came up out of the land of Egypt' — the highway of return: 'a highway' (mesillah, a raised/built road) from Assyria for the remnant (shear ammo, the remnant of his people), just as there was for Israel in the Exodus. The comparison 'as it was for Israel when they came up from Egypt' (ka'asher haytah leYisrael beyom oloto me'erets Mitsrayim) frames the entire second-Exodus as a typological replay of the first. Isaiah 40:3 ('In the desert prepare the way for the LORD; make straight in the wilderness a highway for our God') and Isaiah 35:8-10 develop this highway theme, fulfilled in John the Baptist's ministry (Matthew 3:3) and the return from Babylon.
